route_1_london.title: The Royal Westminster Walk
route_1_london.description: Historic walking tour through London's royal and political heart

stops.name: Big Ben & Palace of Westminster
stops.description: The Elizabeth Tower housing the famous Big Ben bell, part of the UNESCO World Heritage Palace of Westminster. The Gothic architecture dates from the 1834 rebuild after a fire destroyed the original 1040 palace.
tips: Best photographed from Westminster Bridge or Parliament Square
The tower contains 334 steps to reach the belfry
Big Ben refers to the bell, not the clock tower itself
stops.opening_hours: Exterior viewing 24/7; interior tours require advance booking
stops.admission: Free (exterior); Tours £35-45 for UK residents only
stops.transport: Westminster Underground Station (Circle, District, Jubilee lines)

stops.name: Westminster Abbey
stops.description: A Gothic abbey church built by Edward the Confessor in 1065, site of royal coronations since 1066. Final resting place of monarchs, poets, and notable figures including Elizabeth I, Charles Darwin, and Isaac Newton.
tips: Book skip-the-line tickets online to avoid 1-2 hour queues
Visit Poets' Corner to see memorials to Shakespeare, Dickens, and Chaucer
The Coronation Chair has been used in every coronation since 1308
Audio guide included with admission
stops.transport: 10-minute walk from Big Ben via Parliament Square

stops.name: Downing Street
stops.description: No. 10 Downing Street, the official residence and office of the British Prime Minister since 1735. View the famous black door from the security gates on Whitehall.
tips: Can only be viewed from Whitehall gates for security reasons
Best photo opportunities from the gates at the street entrance
Nearby is the Cenotaph war memorial
stops.transport: 5-minute walk from Westminster Abbey via Parliament Street

stops.name: Horse Guards Parade
stops.description: The official ceremonial entrance to St James's Palace and Buckingham Palace. Home to the Household Cavalry and site of the daily Changing of the Guard ceremony.
tips: Changing of the Guard: Mon-Sat at 11am, Sunday at 10am
Walk through the arches to see the parade ground
Free to watch mounted sentries
Churchill War Rooms museum nearby
stops.transport: 3-minute walk from Downing Street via Whitehall

stops.name: Buckingham Palace
stops.description: The official London residence of the British monarch since 1837. The State Rooms are open to visitors during summer, and the Changing of the Guard ceremony attracts thousands daily.
tips: Changing of the Guard at 11am (check schedule - not daily)
Arrive 30 minutes early for good viewing positions
Best viewed from the Victoria Memorial in front
State Rooms open July-September only
The Royal Standard flag flies when the monarch is in residence
stops.transport: 15-minute walk through St James's Park from Horse Guards

stops.name: Trafalgar Square
stops.description: London's most famous public square, built to commemorate Admiral Nelson's 1805 victory at Trafalgar. Features Nelson's Column (169ft), four bronze lions, and the National Gallery.
tips: Climb the steps to the National Gallery for the best square views
The fountains send water jets 24 meters into the air
Four bronze lions by Sir Edwin Landseer guard Nelson's Column
Free WiFi available in the square
Regular cultural events and celebrations held here
stops.transport: 10-minute walk from Buckingham Palace via The Mall and Admiralty Arch

route_1_london.route_notes: This 6-hour walking tour covers approximately 4 miles through central London. The route connects via wide pedestrian-friendly streets and includes multiple rest opportunities. Suitable for all fitness levels. Best started at Westminster Underground Station in the morning to avoid peak crowds at Westminster Abbey.
route_1_london.local_transport: All stops accessible via London Underground. Day travel cards recommended.
food_recommendations: The Sanctuary House (traditional British pub near Westminster Abbey)
Café in the Crypt at St Martin-in-the-Fields (near Trafalgar Square)

route_2_kyoto.title: Kyoto's Hidden Temples
route_2_kyoto.description: Serene temple exploration through Kyoto's historic Higashiyama district
route_2_kyoto.best_time: Spring (cherry blossoms) or Autumn (fall foliage), early morning start recommended

stops.name: Fushimi Inari Taisha
stops.description: The head shrine of over 30,000 Inari shrines across Japan, famous for its thousands of vermillion torii gates forming tunnels up Mount Inari. Dedicated to Inari, the Shinto god of rice, sake, and prosperity.
tips: Arrive before 8am to avoid crowds on the lower trails
The full hike to summit is 4km and takes 2 hours round trip
Stop at Yotsutsuji intersection for panoramic Kyoto views
Free entry - donations welcome
Fox statues throughout (Inari's messengers)
Try local specialties: kitsune udon, inari sushi, grilled mochi
stops.transport: JR Inari Station (5 min from Kyoto Station) or Keihan Fushimi-Inari Station

stops.name: Kiyomizu-dera Temple
stops.description: UNESCO World Heritage temple perched on a hillside, famous for its wooden stage jutting out 13 meters from the main hall, supported entirely without nails. Built in 778 CE and rebuilt in 1633.
tips: Walk up via Chawan-zaka (Teapot Lane) for charming shops
The wooden terrace offers spectacular city views
Drink from Otowa Waterfall's three streams (health, longevity, success)
Visit Jishu Shrine above for matchmaking deity
Special evening illuminations during sakura and autumn seasons
Expect crowds - one of Kyoto's most popular temples
stops.transport: From Fushimi Inari: Keihan train to Kiyomizu-Gojo Station, then 20-min uphill walk

stops.name: Sannenzaka & Ninenzaka Streets
stops.description: Charming preserved streets of traditional wooden machiya houses, teahouses, and artisan shops. Legend says falling on Sannenzaka brings three years of bad luck!
tips: Perfect for photography - especially early morning or evening
Many traditional craft shops selling ceramics, fans, and kimono accessories
Rental kimono shops for traditional photo opportunities
Watch your step on the stone slopes
Hidden cafés and tea houses throughout

stops.name: Yasaka Shrine
stops.description: Important Shinto shrine marking the entrance to the Gion district. Famous for the Gion Matsuri festival (one of Japan's largest) held annually in July.
tips: Beautiful lantern-lit stage, especially stunning at dusk
Free entry to main shrine grounds
Adjacent Maruyama Park is Kyoto's oldest public park
Cherry blossom viewing spot in spring
Gateway to exploring Gion's geisha district
stops.transport: 10-minute walk from Ninenzaka through Higashiyama streets

stops.name: Gion District
stops.description: Kyoto's famous geisha district with traditional wooden machiya houses lining Hanamikoji Street. One of the few remaining places to see geiko and maiko (geisha and apprentice geisha).
tips: Best times to spot geiko/maiko: early evening (5-7pm) as they head to appointments
Walk along Hanamikoji Street and Shirakawa-dori
Visit Kennin-ji Temple (Kyoto's oldest Zen temple) nearby
Do not chase, touch, or block geiko/maiko for photos - be respectful
Explore Pontocho alley for traditional restaurants and atmosphere
Many high-end kaiseki restaurants and traditional ochaya (tea houses)
stops.transport: Adjacent to Yasaka Shrine; Gion-Shijo Station on Keihan Line

route_2_kyoto.route_notes: This 8-hour route covers Kyoto's eastern highlights with moderate walking including uphill sections and stairs. Start early at Fushimi Inari (before 8am) to beat crowds. The route involves train transfers and significant walking through historic neighborhoods. Wear comfortable walking shoes. Spring cherry blossoms and autumn colors provide spectacular scenery.
route_2_kyoto.local_transport: Use Keihan train line between Fushimi Inari and Kiyomizu-Gojo. Consider IC card (ICOCA/Suica) for seamless transit.
food_recommendations: Inari sushi and kitsune udon near Fushimi Inari
Traditional Kyoto cuisine (yudofu, obanzai) in Higashiyama
Gion: upscale kaiseki or casual izakaya options

route_3_paris.title: Romantic Seine Evening
route_3_paris.description: Evening stroll along the Seine from the Eiffel Tower to the Louvre at golden hour
route_3_paris.best_time: Late afternoon to evening for golden hour and illuminated monuments

stops.name: Eiffel Tower
stops.description: Paris's iconic 330-meter iron lattice tower built for the 1889 World's Fair. The most-visited paid monument in the world, offering spectacular views of the City of Light.
tips: Best views from Trocadéro Gardens across the Seine
Book summit tickets weeks in advance online
Hourly light show (sparkle) for 5 minutes after sunset
Three levels to explore - each with different perspectives
Visit at sunset for magical golden hour views
Champagne bar on top floor for special occasions

stops.name: Pont Alexandre III
stops.description: Paris's most ornate bridge, built for the 1900 World Exhibition. Named after Tsar Alexander III of Russia, featuring gilded sculptures, art nouveau lamps, and stunning views of the Eiffel Tower and Grand Palais.
tips: One of the most photographed bridges in Paris
Four golden-winged horses (Fames) at each end
Best photos at golden hour
Walk slowly to appreciate the ornate lampposts and sculptures
Perfect spot for Eiffel Tower photos looking west
stops.transport: 30-minute walk along Seine from Eiffel Tower via Quai Branly and Quai d'Orsay

stops.name: Grand Palais & Petit Palais
stops.description: Twin Beaux-Arts exhibition halls built for the 1900 Exposition Universelle. The Grand Palais features a stunning glass and iron dome; both host rotating art exhibitions and cultural events.
tips: Grand Palais currently closed for renovation (reopening 2025)
Petit Palais museum is free for permanent collections
Beautiful architecture for photography, especially at dusk
Located on Avenue Winston Churchill between Pont Alexandre III and Champs-Élysées
stops.transport: Immediately adjacent to Pont Alexandre III

stops.name: Place de la Concorde
stops.description: Paris's largest square, site of the French Revolution's guillotine where Louis XVI and Marie Antoinette were executed. Now features the 3,300-year-old Luxor Obelisk and ornate fountains.
tips: The 23-meter Egyptian obelisk dates from 1250 BCE
Two monumental fountains inspired by Rome's St. Peter's Square
Gateway to Champs-Élysées on one side, Tuileries on the other
Be careful crossing - very busy traffic circle
Beautiful illumination at night
stops.transport: 10-minute walk from Grand Palais via Avenue Winston Churchill

stops.name: Louvre Museum
stops.description: The world's largest art museum and former royal palace, housing 38,000 works including the Mona Lisa, Venus de Milo, and Winged Victory. The iconic glass pyramid entrance was added in 1989.
tips: Glass Pyramid beautifully illuminated after sunset
Courtyard free to enter and walk through
For museum visit: book timed entry tickets online weeks ahead
Exterior best photographed from Cour Napoleon
Walk through Jardin des Tuileries for Seine river views
Perfect ending point - well-connected by Métro
stops.admission: Courtyard free; Museum €22 (free first Sunday of month)
stops.transport: 15-minute walk from Place de la Concorde through Tuileries Garden; Métro Palais Royal-Musée du Louvre (Lines 1, 7)

route_3_paris.route_notes: This 5-hour romantic evening walk covers 3.5km (2.2 miles) along the Seine's right bank. Flat, easy terrain suitable for all fitness levels. Start at the Eiffel Tower in late afternoon (around 4-5pm) to catch golden hour and see monuments illuminated at dusk. The pathway along the Seine offers continuous beautiful views. Perfect for couples or anyone who loves photography.
route_3_paris.local_transport: Start: Métro Bir-Hakeim or Trocadéro. End: Métro Palais Royal-Musée du Louvre. Consider walking the entire route for the full romantic experience.
food_recommendations: Carette at Place du Trocadéro (open until 11:30pm) for pastries
Café along Champs-Élysées for dinner with a view
Traditional bistros in the 7th arrondissement near Eiffel Tower
Picnic option: grab supplies and enjoy along Seine riverbanks
